title: Build Stunning Landing Pages with Claude Code
description: Four tools, one workflow. Everything you need to make Claude Code build landing pages that actually look good.
intro: Everything you need to make Claude Code build landing pages that actually look good. Four tools, one workflow, zero guesswork.

blocks.content: ShadCN is a component library that gives you clean, production-ready UI components. Instead of building buttons, cards, and navbars from scratch, you pull them in and customize. It's not a framework you install globally. You copy the components you need into your project.
To use it with Claude Code, install the ShadCN MCP server. This lets Claude directly browse and pull components from the ShadCN registry while building your page.
Add this to your Claude Code MCP config (~/.claude/mcp.json or your project's .mcp.json):

Magic UI takes things up a notch. It's built on top of ShadCN and adds animated components, transitions, and effects that make your landing page feel alive. Think: animated gradients, text reveals, marquees, bento grids.
Same deal. Install the Magic UI MCP server so Claude Code can access the full component library while it builds.
This is Claude Code's built-in plugin for frontend work. You've probably seen people talk about it. It gives Claude better context about design patterns, layout principles, and responsive behavior.
Install it from within Claude Code:
Once installed, Claude Code becomes much more intentional about spacing, typography, and visual hierarchy. It stops making things that look like a developer designed them.

This is the secret weapon. The Playwright MCP server gives Claude Code the ability to open a browser, navigate to your dev server, and take screenshots of your landing page. That means it can see what it built, decide if it looks right, and iterate automatically.
No more copy-pasting screenshots into chat. Claude Code just looks at the page itself.
Here's what the actual workflow looks like when you put all four together:

items: Start a new project. Tell Claude Code: "Build me a landing page for [product]. Use ShadCN for the base components and Magic UI for animations."
Claude Code pulls components from both libraries using the MCP servers. It knows what's available because it can browse the registries directly.
The frontend design plugin kicks in to handle layout decisions, spacing, responsive breakpoints, and typography.
Once the first version is built, Playwright MCP opens the page in a browser and screenshots it. Claude Code reviews the screenshot and makes adjustments.
It keeps iterating. Screenshot, evaluate, fix, screenshot again. You end up with something that actually looks polished without manually tweaking CSS for hours.

blocks.content: The key insight: Claude Code isn't guessing what the page looks like. It can see it. That changes everything about the quality of output you get.

proTips.title: Start with ShadCN components, then layer Magic UI on top
proTips.body: Don't try to use both at once from the start. Get your layout and structure right with ShadCN first. Then go back and swap in Magic UI components where you want animation or visual flair. This keeps things clean and avoids conflicts.

proTips.title: Tell Claude Code your dev server URL upfront
proTips.body: When you start the session, tell Claude Code something like "my dev server is running at localhost:3000". That way when Playwright MCP kicks in, it knows exactly where to look. Otherwise it might try to open the wrong URL or ask you.

proTips.title: Use Playwright MCP for mobile views too
proTips.body: You can tell Claude Code to screenshot at different viewport sizes. Ask it to check both desktop and mobile. This catches responsive issues early instead of finding them after you deploy.

proTips.title: Pin your component versions
proTips.body: If you're building something production-grade, pin the ShadCN and Magic UI component versions in your project. MCP servers pull the latest by default, which is great for prototyping but can break things if the API changes.
